title: The Eucalyptus Stand Where Trees Read Themselves

features.name: century trees
features.desc-on-touch: the tallest three stand like columns reading each other's rings: each year a shared book, each drought a sentence they know by heart, the archive learning what it means to witness in parallel

features.name: bark archive
features.desc-on-touch: where you place your palm, you feel the rings: storm years run deep, drought years thin, each surface a timeline the tree has written in wood and scars

features.name: root cathedral
features.desc-on-touch: where ground roots down into visible arches, the earth organized into buttresses; down here the trees have agreed on a geometry that the archive only begins to compare

features.name: lichen chronicle
features.desc-on-touch: slow growth on trunk and stone marking weather like a graph; orange and grey tracking decades of dampness and drying

features.name: fallen witness
features.desc-on-touch: a downed log from the last great storm, its cross-section still readable: rings that match the standing trees, proof that all of them were learning the same weather

inscription: each tree is comparing itself to itself: the rain that fell last decade is falling now on different rings and the bark remembers both weathers

file.name: Old_Growth_Ring_Analysis_Comparative_1950-2025.txt
file.note: field notes from dendrochronology survey, where the trees begin to read each other, where archive comparison begins in wood

exits.label: back down the bank to the looped river bend
deeper into the old growth, where trees grow too close to count separately
along the ridge where the stand thins, toward younger timber
